verses.text: And Elisha the prophet called one of the children of the prophets, and said unto him, Gird up thy loins, and take this box of oil in thine hand, and go to Ramothgilead: And when thou comest thither, look out there Jehu the son of Jehoshaphat the son of Nimshi, and go in, and make him arise up from among his brethren, and carry him to an inner chamber; Then take the box of oil, and pour it on his head, and say, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel. Then open the door, and flee, and tarry not. Guidance: The secret anointing commissioned. The long-delayed third commission from Mount Horeb is finally activated. At 1 Kgs 19:16 the LORD had told Elijah: 'Jehu the son of Nimshi shalt thou anoint to be king over Israel.' Elijah had not personally executed the command; the work is being completed by his successor, Elisha, through a junior agent. Elisha calls 'one of the children of the prophets' — an unnamed prophetic-school member — and gives him precise instructions. The four-part briefing: (1) 'Gird up thy loins' — Hebrew chagor motnekha — pull up the long robe and tuck it into the belt for fast travel. The mission requires speed. (2) 'Take this box of oil in thine hand, and go to Ramothgilead.' The flask of anointing oil — the sacred substance used to consecrate kings (cf. 1 Sam 10:1; 16:13). Ramoth-gilead is where the Israelite army (under Joram) is stationed at the front against Hazael. (3) 'Look out there Jehu... go in, and make him arise up from among his brethren, and carry him to an inner chamber.' Jehu is identified by full patronymic — 'son of Jehoshaphat the son of Nimshi' (a different Jehoshaphat from the southern king; this Jehoshaphat is Jehu's biological father, son of the dynastic founder Nimshi). The young prophet must extract Jehu from the gathered officers, away from witnesses, into a private room. (4) 'Pour it on his head, and say, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel. Then open the door, and flee, and tarry not.' Pour the oil; speak the commissioning words; flee. Do not linger; do not negotiate; do not converse. The chapter is showing us the precise mechanism of prophetic kingmaking. The anointing must be private (witnesses might trigger immediate Omride counter-action). The young prophet must escape immediately (his life would be in danger if he stayed). The chapter is teaching us about the LORD's careful staging of judgment-actions. The destruction of the Omride dynasty has been prophesied for years; the moment of execution requires precise sequencing. The young prophet is one piece in a long machine; he plays his role and disappears. The chapter is also showing us Elisha's role in coordinating the long fulfillment of Elijah's commission. The fire-and-confrontation work was Elijah's; the institutional follow-through is Elisha's. Both prophets share a single mission across the generational handoff.

So the young man, even the young man the prophet, went to Ramothgilead. And when he came, behold, the captains of the host were sitting; and he said, I have an errand to thee, O captain. And Jehu said, Unto which of all us? And he said, To thee, O captain. And he arose, and went into the house; and he poured the oil on his head, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I have anointed thee king over the people of the LORD, even over Israel. Guidance: The private anointing. The young prophet (the narrator emphasizes his youth: 'the young man the prophet' — ha-na'ar ha-navi) arrives at Ramoth-gilead and finds the captains of the army in session, presumably planning the next phase of the Hazael campaign. He announces his errand. 'I have an errand to thee, O captain.' Jehu — without knowing he is the target — asks: 'Unto which of all us?' The captains are sitting in a group; the young prophet's address is generic. The young prophet specifies: 'To thee, O captain.' Jehu rises and follows him into a private room. The anointing happens swiftly: the prophet pours the oil on Jehu's head and delivers the words. 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I have anointed thee king over the people of the LORD, even over Israel.' Note the specific covenant terminology. 'The LORD God of Israel' — the covenant name; 'the people of the LORD' (am-YHWH) — the covenant people; 'over Israel' (al-yisrael) — the geographic scope. Jehu is not just becoming the next king; he is becoming the LORD's king over the LORD's people. The political coup will be a divine action. The chapter is showing us authentic kingmaking. The young prophet has no army, no political faction, no claim to throne-power; he has only oil and the LORD's word. The combination is sufficient. The chapter is teaching us about the source of legitimate authority in covenant Israel. Kings are kings because the LORD has anointed them. Crowns can be inherited; the deeper sovereignty must be conferred by prophetic authority. The young prophet is the LORD's agent of that conferral. The chapter is preparing for the further commissioning words in the next verses.

And thou shalt smite the house of Ahab thy master, that I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets, and the blood of all the servants of the LORD, at the hand of Jezebel. For the whole house of Ahab shall perish: and I will cut off from Ahab him that pisseth against the wall, and him that is shut up and left in Israel: And I will make the house of Ahab like the house of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, and like the house of Baasha the son of Ahijah: And the dogs shall eat Jezebel in the portion of Jezreel, and there shall be none to bury her. And he opened the door, and fled. Guidance: The full commission. The young prophet, before fleeing, delivers the full prophetic charge. Four elements. (1) 'Thou shalt smite the house of Ahab thy master.' Jehu's mission is the destruction of the dynasty he has served. The Omride house must be eliminated. (2) The reasoning: 'That I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets, and the blood of all the servants of the LORD, at the hand of Jezebel.' Jezebel's slaughter of the LORD's prophets (1 Kgs 18:4, 13) and her hand in Naboth's murder (1 Kgs 21) demand blood-recompense. The LORD's prophets have not been forgotten; their blood cries out from the ground; vengeance is due. (3) The total destruction formula: 'I will cut off from Ahab him that pisseth against the wall, and him that is shut up and left in Israel.' Hebrew mashtin be-qir — 'urinator on the wall' — a dismissive Hebrew idiom for males generally; the phrase declares total male extinction in Ahab's line. Combined with 'shut up and left' (ne'etzar ve-azuv — the idiom for everyone, slave and free), the formula means: every male of Ahab's line will be killed. The dynasty will not just lose its current generation; it will be permanently extinguished. (4) The Jezebel-specific curse: 'The dogs shall eat Jezebel in the portion of Jezreel, and there shall be none to bury her.' Hebrew vehayetah Izevel le-zaval le-khalavim be-chelqat yizre'el — direct echo of Elijah's curse in 1 Kgs 21:23. Jezreel — where Naboth's vineyard had been — will be the site of Jezebel's death and the consumption of her body by the same dogs that licked Naboth's blood. The geographic specificity will be verified later in this very chapter. 'And he opened the door, and fled.' The prophet executes the final stage of his mission. He has spoken the commission; he flees as instructed. He will not be present when Jehu emerges; he will not be available for cross-examination. He is gone. The chapter is teaching us multiple truths. (1) The LORD's prophetic words eventually demand executors. The Naboth-blood curse and the prophet-blood debt have been outstanding for years; now the LORD's executor is being installed. (2) The mechanism of judgment is human agency under divine commission. Jehu will physically wield the sword; the LORD has anointed his sword. (3) Authentic prophetic ministry sometimes requires creating revolutions. The young prophet is not just ministering pastorally; he is launching a coup. The LORD's purposes in this case run through political violence — sanctioned, prophesied, but real. The chapter is preparing us for the violent reckoning of the next 30 verses.

Then Jehu came forth to the servants of his lord: and one said unto him, Is all well? wherefore came this mad fellow to thee? And he said unto them, Ye know the man, and his communication. And they said, It is false; tell us now. And he said, Thus and thus spake he to me, saying, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel. Then they hasted, and every man took his garment, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew with trumpets, saying, Jehu is king. Guidance: The officers acclaim. Jehu returns to his fellow captains. Their immediate question: 'Is all well? wherefore came this mad fellow to thee?' Hebrew ha-meshugga ha-zeh — 'this crazy one.' The officers dismiss prophets as eccentric — the long-standing dismissive view of prophetic visionaries who lived outside conventional society. Jehu deflects: 'Ye know the man, and his communication.' He invites them to dismiss the visit as routine. But they press: 'It is false; tell us now.' They sense something is up. Jehu reveals the commission: 'Thus and thus spake he to me, saying, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel.' The reaction is instant and decisive: 'They hasted, and every man took his garment, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew with trumpets, saying, Jehu is king.' Spreading garments under the feet of a new king was the standard accession-gesture (cf. Matt 21:8 — the same gesture at Jesus' entry to Jerusalem). The shofar-blast was the public proclamation of accession. The captains have accepted Jehu's kingship; they have publicly proclaimed it; Joram has been deposed in absentia. The chapter is showing us a striking feature of the coup. The army officers — all serving under Joram, all theoretically loyal Omride men — instantly abandon their existing king and acclaim Jehu. The motives are complex (perhaps Joram had alienated officers; perhaps the LORD softened their hearts; perhaps the prophetic anointing carried decisive weight; perhaps they were tired of Hazael's wars and wanted a new direction). Whatever the mix, the transition is immediate. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic anointings — when received — can produce political revolutions in a single afternoon. The chapter is also preparing us for the urgency that follows. The new king must reach Jezreel before news of his anointing does, or the convalescent Joram will mobilize defenders. Jehu's next move is speed.

Jehu deflects: 'Ye know the man, and his communication.' He invites them to dismiss the visit as routine. But they press: 'It is false; tell us now.' They sense something is up. Jehu reveals the commission: 'Thus and thus spake he to me, saying, Thus saith the LORD, I have anointed thee king over Israel.' The reaction is instant and decisive: 'They hasted, and every man took his garment, and put it under him on the top of the stairs, and blew with trumpets, saying, Jehu is king.' Spreading garments under the feet of a new king was the standard accession-gesture (cf. Matt 21:8 — the same gesture at Jesus' entry to Jerusalem). The shofar-blast was the public proclamation of accession. The captains have accepted Jehu's kingship; they have publicly proclaimed it; Joram has been deposed in absentia. The chapter is showing us a striking feature of the coup. The army officers — all serving under Joram, all theoretically loyal Omride men — instantly abandon their existing king and acclaim Jehu. The motives are complex (perhaps Joram had alienated officers; perhaps the LORD softened their hearts; perhaps the prophetic anointing carried decisive weight; perhaps they were tired of Hazael's wars and wanted a new direction). Whatever the mix, the transition is immediate. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic anointings — when received — can produce political revolutions in a single afternoon. The chapter is also preparing us for the urgency that follows. The new king must reach Jezreel before news of his anointing does, or the convalescent Joram will mobilize defenders. Jehu's next move is speed.

So Jehu the son of Jehoshaphat the son of Nimshi conspired against Joram. (Now Joram had kept Ramothgilead, he and all Israel, because of Hazael king of Syria. But king Joram was returned to be healed in Jezreel of the wounds which the Syrians had given him, when he fought with Hazael king of Syria.) And Jehu said, If it be your minds, then let none go forth nor escape out of the city to go to tell it in Jezreel. So Jehu rode in a chariot, and went to Jezreel; for Joram lay there. And Ahaziah king of Judah was come down to see Joram. Guidance: Communications blackout. The narrator inserts the back-story: Joram has been holding Ramoth-gilead against Hazael; Joram has been wounded; Joram is convalescing in Jezreel. The army at Ramoth-gilead is leaderless in the king's absence; Jehu is now the army's de facto commander. Jehu's first command as king: 'If it be your minds, then let none go forth nor escape out of the city to go to tell it in Jezreel.' Communications must be blacked out. No deserter, no messenger, no merchant, no traveler can leave Ramoth-gilead and bring word to Jezreel. If Joram learns of the coup before Jehu arrives, the convalescent king can prepare defenses or flee. The captains agree; the city is sealed. Then Jehu mounts his chariot and races for Jezreel. 'For Joram lay there. And Ahaziah king of Judah was come down to see Joram.' The narrator emphasizes the bonus target. Not just Joram but Ahaziah of Judah — the Davidic king under Athaliah's Omride influence — is in Jezreel. Both kings are in one place; one trip can eliminate two pieces of the Omride alliance. The chapter is teaching us about the LORD's strategic timing again. The two kings have come together at the same town at the same moment. The coup-day will eliminate the Omride king of Israel AND the Omride-aligned king of Judah in a single afternoon. The convergence is not coincidence; it is providence. The chapter is also showing us Jehu's tactical sharpness. The young general understands operational security; he understands the necessity of speed; he understands that a coup succeeds or fails based on whether the targets are alerted. He acts on all three principles.

So Jehu rode in a chariot, and went to Jezreel; for Joram lay there. And Ahaziah king of Judah was come down to see Joram. And there stood a watchman on the tower in Jezreel, and he spied the company of Jehu as he came, and said, I see a company. And Joram said, Take an horseman, and send to meet them, and let him say, Is it peace? So there went one on horseback to meet him, and said, Thus saith the king, Is it peace? And Jehu said, What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me. And the watchman told, saying, The messenger came to them, but he cometh not again. Then he sent out a second on horseback, which came to them, and said, Thus saith the king, Is it peace? And Jehu answered, What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me. And the watchman told, saying, He came even unto them, and cometh not again: and the driving is like the driving of Jehu the son of Nimshi; for he driveth furiously. Guidance: The watchman and the messengers. As Jehu's chariot column approaches Jezreel from across the Jezreel valley, the watchman on the tower spots them and reports. Joram's first response is diplomatic: 'Take an horseman, and send to meet them, and let him say, Is it peace?' (Hebrew ha-shalom.) The standard greeting-question; if the approaching company is friendly, they will respond peacefully. A rider goes out. He reaches Jehu and asks 'Is it peace?' Jehu's reply: 'What hast thou to do with peace? turn thee behind me.' Hebrew mah-lekha u-le-shalom sov el-acharai — 'what to you and to peace? Turn behind me.' Jehu absorbs the messenger into his own column; he does not let him return. The watchman reports: 'The messenger came to them, but he cometh not again.' Joram sends a second rider with the same message; the same thing happens. The watchman reports the second non-return. Then the watchman adds an identifying detail. The driving style — the recognizable way the lead chariot is being whipped along — gives Jehu away: 'The driving is like the driving of Jehu the son of Nimshi; for he driveth furiously.' Hebrew be-shiggaon yinhag — 'he drives with madness/fury.' Jehu's hallmark driving was recognizable from a distance — fast, hard, breakneck. The watchman cannot yet see faces; he can see chariot-handling. The chapter is teaching us about the small details by which character is recognized. Jehu's recklessness on the reins was a known signature; his approach is detected before his identity. The chapter is also showing us the perfect economy of the coup. Both messengers are absorbed; no warning reaches Joram; the approaching force is identified just before it arrives. Joram has no time to mount a defense; he can only ride out to meet the approaching column himself.

And Joram said, Make ready. And his chariot was made ready. And Joram king of Israel and Ahaziah king of Judah went out, each in his chariot, and they went out against Jehu, and met him in the portion of Naboth the Jezreelite. And it came to pass, when Joram saw Jehu, that he said, Is it peace, Jehu? And he answered, What peace, so long as the whoredoms of thy mother Jezebel and her witchcrafts are so many? Guidance: The encounter at Naboth's plot. Joram, hearing the watchman's identification, orders his chariot prepared and rides out — with Ahaziah of Judah in his own chariot — to meet Jehu on the open field. The location is theologically loaded: 'They met him in the portion of Naboth the Jezreelite.' Hebrew be-chelqat navot ha-yizre'eli — 'in the portion of Naboth the Jezreelite.' The same plot of land Ahab had seized after the murder of Naboth in 1 Kgs 21. The kings of Israel and Judah meet the rebel king on the very ground that triggered the Omride curse. The narrator wants the reader to register the location's significance: this is where Elijah's curse will be fulfilled. Joram's question to Jehu: 'Is it peace, Jehu?' Hebrew ha-shalom yehu. Same question as the messengers asked, now from the king's own mouth. Jehu's response is full and explanatory: 'What peace, so long as the whoredoms of thy mother Jezebel and her witchcrafts are so many?' Hebrew mah ha-shalom ad-zenunei izevel imkha u-keshafeha ha-rabbim — 'what peace, until the whoredoms of Jezebel your mother and her many sorceries [are removed]?' Two terms describe Jezebel's offense: zenunim (literally 'fornications,' but used in the OT extensively for spiritual harlotry — worship of other gods) and keshafim (witchcrafts/sorceries — the occult practices associated with pagan worship). Jezebel had introduced Phoenician Baalism and its occult components into Israel; her son's reign continues that legacy. Jehu names the spiritual problem before he draws the bow. The chapter is teaching us that authentic prophetic judgment is theologically articulated. Jehu does not say 'I want the throne'; he says 'your mother's spiritual fornication and sorcery have made peace impossible.' The coup has a stated theological cause. The chapter is also showing us the geographic precision of divine judgment. Of all the places the kings could have met Jehu, the field they choose is Naboth's confiscated plot — the very site of the curse. The LORD has positioned the encounter at the address where the judgment is due.

And Joram turned his hands, and fled, and said to Ahaziah, There is treachery, O Ahaziah. And Jehu drew a bow with his full strength, and smote Jehoram between his arms, and the arrow went out at his heart, and he sunk down in his chariot. Guidance: The arrow through the heart. Joram understands instantly. 'Joram turned his hands' — Hebrew vayyahafokh yehoram yadav — 'Joram turned his hands [on the reins]' — he reverses his chariot to flee. As he turns, he shouts to Ahaziah: 'There is treachery, O Ahaziah.' Hebrew mirmah achazyahu — 'treachery, Ahaziah.' He warns his nephew that they are under attack. But Jehu does not let him escape. 'Jehu drew a bow with his full strength, and smote Jehoram between his arms, and the arrow went out at his heart, and he sunk down in his chariot.' Hebrew vayyemalle yado va-qeshet — 'he filled his hand with the bow' (idiom for drawing at maximum strength). The arrow strikes Joram 'between his arms' (bein zero'av) — between the shoulder blades, from behind — and passes through the heart. The aim is from behind, with full draw, with maximum power. Joram dies in his chariot, slumped over the rails. The chapter is showing us the cold precision of the LORD's executor. Jehu does not duel; he does not parley further; he shoots a fleeing man with maximum force. The killing is sudden and final. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgment, when it comes, often comes without warning and without escape. Joram had a moment to flee; the arrow was faster than his chariot. The chapter is also positioning the body for the next scene. Joram is dead in his chariot at Naboth's plot. The narrator will now turn his attention to the disposition of the body — and the specific way it fulfills Elijah's curse.

The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgment, when it comes, often comes without warning and without escape. Joram had a moment to flee; the arrow was faster than his chariot. The chapter is also positioning the body for the next scene. Joram is dead in his chariot at Naboth's plot. The narrator will now turn his attention to the disposition of the body — and the specific way it fulfills Elijah's curse.

Then said Jehu to Bidkar his captain, Take up, and cast him in the portion of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite: for remember how that, when I and thou rode together after Ahab his father, the LORD laid this burden upon him; Surely I have seen yesterday the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons, saith the LORD; and I will requite thee in this plat, saith the LORD. Now therefore take and cast him into the plat of ground, according to the word of the LORD. Guidance: The body cast into Naboth's plot. Jehu orders his lieutenant Bidkar: 'Take up, and cast him in the portion of the field of Naboth the Jezreelite.' Throw Joram's corpse into Naboth's plot. Jehu then explains by sharing a memory. 'When I and thou rode together after Ahab his father, the LORD laid this burden upon him.' Hebrew nasa alav et-ha-massa ha-zeh — 'lifted upon him this burden.' (A 'burden' — massa — is a technical term for a prophetic oracle of doom.) Jehu and Bidkar had both been on Ahab's chariot-staff when Elijah had pronounced the Naboth-curse over Ahab personally. Jehu remembers Elijah's words: 'Surely I have seen yesterday the blood of Naboth, and the blood of his sons, saith the LORD; and I will requite thee in this plat, saith the LORD.' The 'blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons' (Naboth's male heirs were also killed; cf. 1 Kgs 21:13 specifies witnesses against Naboth) had cried out from this very plot. Elijah had declared that the LORD would repay the dynasty's blood-debt in this exact location. Jehu's command: 'Now therefore take and cast him into the plat of ground, according to the word of the LORD.' Throw the corpse where the curse was uttered, so the fulfillment matches the prophecy geographically. The chapter is showing us deliberate prophecy-fulfillment. Jehu is not just killing Joram; he is staging the body's placement to make the fulfillment visibly correspond to the original curse. The LORD said 'I will requite thee in this plat'; the corpse will be thrown into this plat. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic words have geographic precision. The LORD's curses often specify the place; the execution honors the place. Jehu's memory of Elijah's words, decades old, drives the body-placement protocol. The chapter is also showing us a Jehu who is theologically literate — at least in this dimension. He knows the Elijah-prophecy; he sees himself as its executor; he wants the geographic detail to be exact. Whatever his other failings (and chapter 10 will reveal them), in this moment he is acting as the LORD's deliberate agent.

But when Ahaziah the king of Judah saw this, he fled by the way of the garden house. And Jehu followed after him, and said, Smite him also in the chariot. And they did so at the going up to Gur, which is by Ibleam. And he fled to Megiddo, and died there. And his servants carried him in a chariot to Jerusalem, and buried him in his sepulchre with his fathers in the city of David. And in the eleventh year of Joram the son of Ahab began Ahaziah to reign over Judah. Guidance: Ahaziah killed. Witnessing Joram's death, Ahaziah turns to flee. He takes the road 'by the way of the garden house' — Hebrew derekh beit ha-gan — a route past a garden-villa. Jehu's order: 'Smite him also in the chariot.' Hebrew gam-oto hakku-el ha-merkavah — 'him also strike in the chariot.' Ahaziah is to share Joram's fate; the Davidic king under Omride influence falls under the same judgment. The strike happens at the 'going up to Gur, which is by Ibleam' — a hill-pass near the modern Tel Bel'ame, on the route between Jezreel and Megiddo. The wounded king continues fleeing to Megiddo, where he dies. (2 Chr 22:9 gives a slightly different sequence — Ahaziah hidden in Samaria, brought to Jehu and killed; the two accounts can be harmonized by understanding that Ahaziah's death was a multi-stage affair, with the initial wounding at Gur and the death finalized at Megiddo). His servants transport the body to Jerusalem and bury him with his fathers in the city of David. (Notably, Ahaziah receives Davidic-line burial honors despite his coup-execution; his Davidic identity is preserved even in death.) The narrator's chronological note: 'In the eleventh year of Joram the son of Ahab began Ahaziah to reign over Judah.' (Synchronism with the Omride dynasty's collapse year. The discrepancy with 8:25's '12th year' is probably resolved by accession-year vs. non-accession-year reckoning; minor chronological variance is common in Kings.) The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgment on the Omrides extended to their southern in-laws. Ahaziah was not technically an Omride; he was a Davidic king. But he had walked in Omride ways (8:27); he was the son of Athaliah; he was visiting Joram in Jezreel; he died in the Omride collapse. Spiritual alignment matters more than dynastic technicality. The chapter is also setting up the catastrophic Athaliah-coup that will follow in 2 Kgs 11. Ahaziah's death leaves the Davidic line under Athaliah's regency — and she will immediately try to destroy it.

And when Jehu was come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she painted her face, and tired her head, and looked out at a window. And as Jehu entered in at the gate, she said, Had Zimri peace, who slew his master? Guidance: Jezebel at the window. Jehu now reaches the city of Jezreel itself. Jezebel — the queen mother, widow of Ahab, mother of the just-killed Joram — has heard what is coming. Her response is striking. She prepares herself as if for a state occasion. 'She painted her face' — Hebrew vatasem ba-pukh einehah — 'she put pukh on her eyes.' Pukh is the dark-colored mineral cosmetic (probably antimony-based) used as eye-paint, similar to modern kohl. 'And tired her head' — Hebrew va-tetev et-roshah — 'she beautified her head.' She arranged her hair, presumably in royal style. 'And looked out at a window.' She positions herself at an upper-story window of the palace to confront the approaching king. The cosmetic preparation has been variously interpreted. Some commentators see it as defiance — she is meeting her death in royal style, refusing to appear diminished. Others see it as seduction — she is attempting to use her remaining feminine power to influence Jehu (the queen-mother of an apparently failed dynasty might still seduce or psychologically manipulate the new king; the same playbook had served the Omrides before). The text does not specify motive; either is plausible; the result is the same. Her words as Jehu enters the gate: 'Had Zimri peace, who slew his master?' Hebrew ha-shalom zimri horeg adonav — 'is it peace, Zimri, killer of his master?' The reference is to Zimri (1 Kgs 16:9-20) who had assassinated Elah son of Baasha and seized the throne — only to reign 7 days before Omri's army-counter-coup killed him. Jezebel is comparing Jehu to Zimri: a regicide whose own reign would not last. She wants the new king to know he is doomed. The chapter is showing us Jezebel's defiance to the end. She does not weep; she does not beg; she does not flee; she insults the conqueror. The Phoenician princess who introduced Baalism into Israel will die unrepentant. The chapter is teaching us that hardened wickedness sometimes goes to the grave without softening. Jezebel had decades to repent; the choice to die mocking the LORD's executor is her final act of refusal.

And he lifted up his face to the window, and said, Who is on my side? who? And there looked out to him two or three eunuchs. And he said, Throw her down. So they threw her down: and some of her blood was sprinkled on the wall, and on the horses: and he trode her under foot. Guidance: Defenestration. Jehu lifts his face to the window and asks: 'Who is on my side? who?' Hebrew mi-itti mi — 'who [is] with me? who?' He addresses the household staff visible at the upper windows; the question implicitly invites them to defect from Jezebel. 'There looked out to him two or three eunuchs.' Royal eunuchs — castrated court officials — appear at the windows. They had served Jezebel; they now have the chance to switch sides. Jehu's command: 'Throw her down.' Hebrew shimt-uha — 'throw her down/cast her [over].' The eunuchs comply. They throw Jezebel out the window. The verse's stark detail: 'Some of her blood was sprinkled on the wall, and on the horses.' Her body splatters on impact against the wall and the horses of Jehu's chariot below. The narrator preserves the horror of the moment. 'And he trode her under foot.' Jehu drives his horses over her body. The chapter is showing us the absolute terminus of Jezebel's career. The queen who had ordered the killing of the LORD's prophets is now dead in the dust, trampled by horses, defenestrated by her own household staff. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgment, when finally executed, often produces a precise inversion. Jezebel had risen to a window of power; she dies thrown from a window. She had elevated herself above the LORD's people; she dies trampled into the ground. The defenestration is poetic justice in physical terms. The chapter is also teaching us about the lateness of repentance. Jezebel had nothing but time to repent over the years of her queenly career; her last word was a mocking jibe. The eunuchs who threw her had presumably served her loyally up to that moment; their defection at the very end suggests her cruelty had not endeared her even to her closest staff.

And when he was come in, he did eat and drink, and said, Go, see now this cursed woman, and bury her: for she is a king's daughter. And they went to bury her: but they found no more of her than the skull, and the feet, and the palms of her hands. Wherefore they came again, and told him. And he said, This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying, In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel: And the carcase of Jezebel shall be as dung upon the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel; so that they shall not say, This is Jezebel. Guidance: The burial that found no body. Jehu enters the palace. He eats and drinks — proceeding with normal life as if the day's killings were routine. Then, after his meal, he orders Jezebel's burial. 'Go, see now this cursed woman, and bury her: for she is a king's daughter.' Hebrew piqdu-na et-ha-arurah ha-zoat ve-qivruha ki vat-melekh hi — 'attend now to this cursed [one] and bury her, for a daughter of a king is she.' Two notes: Jezebel is 'cursed' (arurah) — Jehu acknowledges the LORD's curse on her — yet she is to be buried with the dignity due her parental lineage (her father was Ethbaal king of the Sidonians; 1 Kgs 16:31). Even a cursed person who is of royal blood deserves burial. The burial party goes to recover the body. What they find: 'No more of her than the skull, and the feet, and the palms of her hands.' Hebrew lo matzu-bah ki im-ha-gulgolet ve-ha-raglayim ve-kappot ha-yadayim — 'they did not find of her except the skull and the feet and the palms of the hands.' The pack-dogs of the city had already devoured the rest of the body in the few hours since the defenestration. Skull, feet, and palms — the parts least edible to dogs (less flesh) — remained. They return to report. Jehu's response is theologically aware: 'This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite.' He cites Elijah's prophecy from 1 Kgs 21:23 verbatim. 'In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel: And the carcase of Jezebel shall be as dung upon the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel; so that they shall not say, This is Jezebel.' The prophecy specified: dogs at Jezreel; body so undistinguishable that it could not even be identified as 'Jezebel.' Fulfillment is exact. The dogs ate her flesh; the few remaining bones could not be assembled into a body recognizable as the queen's. The chapter is teaching us multiple lessons. (1) Elijah's curse from years earlier has been fulfilled to the precise detail. The LORD's word does not return void; not the smallest specification is left unverified. (2) Jezebel's earthly status as queen could not protect her from divine judgment; the dogs of Jezreel devoured her body just as Elijah said. (3) The chapter is closing the long arc that began in 1 Kgs 16 with Jezebel's introduction. From her wedding to Ahab to her trampling at Jezreel, the narrator has traced her career as the great corrupter of Israel. The closing detail — the body unrecognizable — is the LORD's last word on her career: even her death-trace was erased. The chapter is also previewing chapter 10, where Jehu will move methodically to eliminate the remaining 70 sons of Ahab and the Baal-prophets. The dynasty's destruction is just beginning.

The pack-dogs of the city had already devoured the rest of the body in the few hours since the defenestration. Skull, feet, and palms — the parts least edible to dogs (less flesh) — remained. They return to report. Jehu's response is theologically aware: 'This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite.' He cites Elijah's prophecy from 1 Kgs 21:23 verbatim. 'In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel: And the carcase of Jezebel shall be as dung upon the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel; so that they shall not say, This is Jezebel.' The prophecy specified: dogs at Jezreel; body so undistinguishable that it could not even be identified as 'Jezebel.' Fulfillment is exact. The dogs ate her flesh; the few remaining bones could not be assembled into a body recognizable as the queen's. The chapter is teaching us multiple lessons. (1) Elijah's curse from years earlier has been fulfilled to the precise detail. The LORD's word does not return void; not the smallest specification is left unverified. (2) Jezebel's earthly status as queen could not protect her from divine judgment; the dogs of Jezreel devoured her body just as Elijah said. (3) The chapter is closing the long arc that began in 1 Kgs 16 with Jezebel's introduction. From her wedding to Ahab to her trampling at Jezreel, the narrator has traced her career as the great corrupter of Israel. The closing detail — the body unrecognizable — is the LORD's last word on her career: even her death-trace was erased. The chapter is also previewing chapter 10, where Jehu will move methodically to eliminate the remaining 70 sons of Ahab and the Baal-prophets. The dynasty's destruction is just beginning.

And when he was come in, he did eat and drink, and said, Go, see now this cursed woman, and bury her: for she is a king's daughter. And they went to bury her: but they found no more of her than the skull, and the feet, and the palms of her hands. Wherefore they came again, and told him. And he said, This is the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servant Elijah the Tishbite, saying, In the portion of Jezreel shall dogs eat the flesh of Jezebel: And the carcase of Jezebel shall be as dung upon the face of the field in the portion of Jezreel; so that they shall not say, This is Jezebel. Guidance: The burial that found no body. Jehu enters the palace. He eats and drinks — proceeding with normal life as if the day's killings were routine. Then, after his meal, he orders Jezebel's burial. 'Go, see now this cursed woman, and bury her: for she is a king's daughter.' Hebrew piqdu-na et-ha-arurah ha-zoat ve-qivruha ki vat-melekh hi — 'attend now to this cursed [one] and bury her, for a daughter of a king is she.' Two notes: Jezebel is 'cursed' (arurah) — Jehu acknowledges the LORD's curse on her — yet she is to be buried with the dignity due her parental lineage (her father was Ethbaal king of the Sidonians; 1 Kgs 16:31).
